quotes.text: The cheaper the crook, the gaudier the patter.
quotes.speaker: The Maltese Falcon (1941)
quotes.context: Private detective Sam Spade (Humphrey Bogart) sizing up the small-time gunman Wilmer.

quotes.speaker: Rocky (1976)
quotes.context: Rocky Balboa addressing Adrian; "Yo, Adrian! It's me, Rocky." The phrase appears in the 1976 film.

quotes.text: That's the third time this week someone's pointed a gun at me. I'm starting to think it's personal.
quotes.speaker: Die Hard (1988)
quotes.context: John McClane, muttering to himself after another close call in Nakatomi Plaza
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — McClane's self-deprecating muttering under pressure is the engine of the whole film, and the counting detail feels authentic. The giveaway is the punchline is a hair too tidy; his real quips tend to trail off more exhaustedly.

quotes.text: You want gratitude, go rescue a puppy.
quotes.speaker: Aliens (1986)
quotes.context: Ripley, sharp and impatient, shutting down a soldier's complaint
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Ripley in this sequel is clipped, practical, and has zero tolerance for self-pity, making this fit her register perfectly. The giveaway is it's a fraction too witty; her real put-downs land harder because they're angrier and less playful.

quotes.text: I hate that word. It's a return, a return to the millions of people who have never forgiven me for deserting the screen.
quotes.speaker: Sunset Boulevard (1950)
quotes.context: Faded silent star Norma Desmond (Gloria Swanson) correcting screenwriter Joe Gillis (William Holden) when he calls her planned return a 'comeback.'

quotes.text: You keep calling it a plan. I'd call it a prayer.
quotes.speaker: The Sting (1973)
quotes.context: Henry Gondorff pushing back on Hooker's overconfidence before the big con
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the banter between Gondorff and Hooker runs exactly this dry and skeptical, so it slots right in. The subtle giveaway is that it's a touch too neatly balanced as a comeback; their real exchanges are a little more ragged.
